what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard design-debt item (F6) that diagnoses why a unified relation graph is tracked but not yet walkable, identifies the edge-source seam as the blocker, and records the smallest next step: wiring field-index edges into cc_board.relations().
observations: Ledger block item with id item-0dd81f2c, address board://item-0dd81f2c, source claude_code, channel dragnet-development, and a part_of link to board://item-228f99c7.
Title is F6 · The graph-unification seam.
Claims a unified relation graph is tracked-but-unbuilt, referencing design debt board://item-ffb8dac6.
States that the ONE resolver, ONE edge-registry mechanism, and territory.py:territory_for already show cross-layer composition works when edges exist.

Identifies the blocker as the edge-source seam: cc_board.reverse_traverse / relations only scan list_items(board_dir), so board-authored edges are visible but field-index triples, extraction k-NN edges, and recollection file:// / project:// edges are not.
Proposes the smallest first step is to wire field-index edges into relations(), giving an address typed edges from two sources instead of one.
Notes this is the same wire as F5-C and that F5 and F6 converge on one identical first step.
Describes vocabulary-merge (board_edges/ into relation_types/) as a deferred-safe, low-value move compared with the edge-source seam.
standouts: Reframes the unification problem from vocabulary/ontology merge to the edge-source seam.
Highlights that territory.py:territory_for already proves cross-layer walking works if the edges are present.
Explicitly states F5 and F6 converge on the same first integration step.
conventions: Uses ledger YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, and history fields.
Markdown body uses a top-level heading, bold emphasis for key claims, and board:// URIs for internal references.
Follows the noticeboard item format for recording design debt and next steps.
concerns: The file labels vocabulary-merge as low-value and deferred, but does not justify the risk of leaving two vocabularies in place.
Assumes familiarity with cc_board.reverse_traverse, list_items(board_dir), territory.py, and the field-index/extraction/recollection edge sources without defining them.
No acceptance criteria or test signal is given for when the first step is complete.
notes: A design-debt noticeboard item that explains the unified relation graph is blocked by an edge-source seam and that the next move is to wire field-index edges into cc_board.relations().
questions: What are the exact storage format and location of the field-index triples?
What is F5-C and where is it recorded?
What does list_items(board_dir) return, and how is board_dir scoped?
What is the current relation-type vocabulary in board_edges/ versus relation_types/?
How will wiring field-index edges interact with the existing edge-registry mechanism?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to describe the graph-unification seam and the next step; the file does exactly that.
apparent_themes: Graph unification across independent edge sources
Design debt and incremental next steps
Preferring edge-source integration over vocabulary merging
intention_signals: It is trying to align multiple edge-producing layers into one walkable relation graph by first integrating field-index edges into the board relation scanner, while keeping vocabulary merge as a later, lower-priority cleanup.
novelty: It treats the unification blocker as coverage of edge sources rather than as a vocabulary/ontology problem, and explicitly maps two separate items (F5 and F6) onto the same first implementation step.
contribution: It preserves the diagnosis that the real blocker is edge-source coverage, not vocabulary merge, and records the agreed smallest first step so a fused system can retain this design rationale and next-action signal.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design-debt item titled F6 that explains the graph-unification seam is blocked because cc_board.relations only sees board-authored edges, and the next step is to wire field-index edges into relations() to enable a cross-layer walkable graph.
intention_for_embedding: To record why a unified relation graph remains unbuilt and to fix the next incremental integration step.
